target.title: feat(slack): add ignoreOtherMentions channel config
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `ignoreOtherMentions` option for Slack channel config, mirroring the existing Discord guild/channel feature
- When enabled, messages that mention another user (`<@U...>`) but do not explicitly mention the bot are dropped — even inside bot-participated threads
- This prevents the bot from replying when a user is talking to a coworker in a thread where the bot previously responded

## Slack vs Discord difference

Discord's `ignoreOtherMentions` checks `!wasMentioned && !implicitMention`. The Slack version intentionally omits the `!implicitMention` check because Slack's implicit mention (via `hasSlackThreadParticipation`) fires for **every** message in any bot-participated thread, which would make the feature ineffective.

## Config example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      channels: {
        "*": { ignoreOtherMentions: true },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.slack.ts` | Add `ignoreOtherMentions` to `SlackChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` | Add zod validation for the new field |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/channel-config.ts` | Wire through type + resolver |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` | Drop logic before mention gating |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/slack/src/monitor` — 236 tests pass
- [x] Manual test on live Slack workspace: bot no longer replies when tagging a coworker in a bot thread
- [x] Bot still replies when explicitly @mentioned in the same thread

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: fix(google): keep parallel Gemini tool responses in the turn after the model
target.body:
## Summary

On Gemini < 3 vision models (gemini-2.5-*), a single assistant turn with parallel tool calls where a non-last result returns an image corrupts the request so the next call returns 400 INVALID_ARGUMENT. Because the malformed turn is persisted in session history, every subsequent turn re-serializes it and re-400s, so the session is stuck until the user compacts or starts over.

Root cause is in `convertMessages` (`src/llm/providers/google-shared.ts`). Gemini's contract: the user turn immediately following a model turn with N functionCall parts must carry all N matching functionResponse parts, or the API returns 400 (confirmed against gemini-cli #16135 and litellm #26755). For Gemini < 3 (no multimodal functionResponse) an image-bearing tool result emits a separate "Tool result image:" user turn. The old merge heuristic only inspected `contents[contents.length - 1]`: when that separate image turn lands between two parallel function responses, the heuristic sees the image turn (not a functionResponse turn) and starts a fresh user turn for the second response. The turn right after the model then holds only 1 of 2 responses, so the count mismatches and Gemini 400s. It is order-dependent: if the image-bearing result is last it merges fine, which is why it slipped through.

Produced `contents` before the fix: `[user, model(fc1,fc2), user(fr1), user(image), user(fr2)]`, with fr2 stranded behind the image turn.

## Fix

Replace the fragile `contents[last]` heuristic with a run-scoped accumulator. All function responses for one model turn merge into the single user turn right after it; Gemini < 3 image turns are deferred and flushed at the end of the tool-result run so they trail the merged response turn. After the fix: `[user, model(fc1,fc2), user(fr1,fr2), user(image)]`, both responses in the immediately following turn.

Both Gemini surfaces, `google.ts` (Gemini API) and `google-vertex.ts` (Cloud Code Assist / Vertex), route through this single `convertMessages`, so the one change covers both. This is a latent bug in the Gemini-<3 separate-image-turn path (landed around 2026-05-31), not a fresh regression.

## Verification

Local, from repo root:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  src/llm/providers/google-shared.parallel-image-repro.test.ts \
  src/llm/providers/google-shared.convert.test.ts \
  src/llm/providers/google-shared.test.ts
```

Before (production file restored to origin/main, regression test kept):

```
 × keeps both parallel function responses in the user turn immediately after the model turn
   AssertionError: expected 1 to be 2
```

After (with fix):

```
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  14 passed (14)
```

oxlint clean on both changed files.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Gemini < 3 parallel tool-call turn with a non-last image result split function responses across user turns, producing a response-count mismatch that 400s and, because history is persisted, keeps 400-ing on every later turn (stuck session).

Real environment tested: local repo checkout at current origin/main, Node 22.19, Vitest unit config; the conversion path is a pure function that builds the request payload, so no network call is involved.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/llm/providers/google-shared.parallel-image-repro.test.ts src/llm/providers/google-shared.convert.test.ts src/llm/providers/google-shared.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: 3 files passed, 14 tests passed (new repro test plus 11 convert and 2 google-shared regression tests). Reverting only the production file to origin/main while keeping the test reproduces the failure `expected 1 to be 2`, confirming the test fails without the fix and passes with it.

Observed result after fix: the user turn immediately after the model carries both function responses; the "Tool result image:" turn trails it; no function response is stranded in any later turn.

What was not tested: no live Gemini 2.5 API round-trip (would need gemini-2.5 credentials). Proof is through the deterministic `convertMessages` payload builder that produces the exact `contents` array sent to the API.

target.title: fix(telegram): clarify inbound document fallback reason
target.body:
## Summary

* Problem: Inbound Telegram document download failures could degrade into a placeholder-only body like `<media:document>` with no explanation.
* Why it matters: This makes attachment failures ambiguous and harder for the agent to interpret, especially for Bot API size-limit cases.
* What changed: Preserve `<media:document>` for failed inbound document downloads and append an explicit reason line for `file too large` vs `download failed`.
* What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add support for downloading Telegram files over the Bot API size limit and does not change proxy, timeout, SSRF, or unrelated media behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

* [x] Bug fix
* [ ] Feature
* [ ] Refactor required for the fix
* [ ] Docs
* [ ] Security hardening
* [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

* [ ] Gateway / orchestration
* [ ] Skills / tool execution
* [ ] Auth / tokens
* [ ] Memory / storage
* [x] Integrations
* [ ] API / contracts
* [ ] UI / DX
* [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

* Related #55917
* [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

* Root cause: Failed inbound Telegram document downloads could fall back to placeholder-only output without surfacing why the attachment was unavailable.
* Missing detection / guardrail: Existing coverage did not lock in the final rendered body behavior for failed inbound document downloads.
* Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Scoped to the Telegram inbound document fallback path discussed in #55917.
* Why this regressed now: The fallback path preserved message delivery, but not enough user-visible context to explain attachment unavailability.
* If unknown, what was ruled out: Ruled out broader Telegram transport, proxy, timeout, SSRF, and Bot API size-limit behavior changes as part of this fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

* Coverage level that should have caught this:

  * [x] Unit test
  * [x] Seam / integration test
  * [ ] End-to-end test
  * [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
* Target test or file:

  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.resolve-media-retry.test.ts`
  * `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.media-fallback.test.ts`
* Scenario the test should lock in:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * failed document download returns an explicit fallback reason
  * file-too-large and generic download failure are distinguished
  * final rendered body keeps `<media:document>` and includes the unavailable reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug spans both resolver behavior and final body rendering, so focused resolver coverage plus one final body-format test is the smallest reliable guardrail.
* Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing retry coverage covered related resolver behavior, but not final body rendering for failed inbound document downloads.
* If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

* Failed inbound Telegram document downloads no longer degrade into a silent placeholder-only body.
* Failed inbound documents still preserve `<media:document>`.
* The final message body now includes one explicit reason line:

  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: file too large for Bot API download`
  * `Telegram attachment unavailable: download failed`
* Successful inbound document downloads remain unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Telegram document download fails] -> [body becomes <media:document>] -> [failure reason is unclear]

After:
[inbound Telegram document download fails]
  -> [keep <media:document>]
  -> [append explicit unavailable reason]
  -> [agent sees both document placeholder and failure reason]
```

## Security Impact (required)

* New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
* Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
* Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
* If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

* OS: Windows 10
* Runtime/container: local dev environment
* Model/provider: N/A
* Integration/channel (if any): Telegram extension logic
* Relevant config (redacted): standard local repo/test setup

### Steps

1. Receive an inbound Telegram document message.
2. Hit a document download failure in the existing fallback path.
3. Build the final message body for agent ingestion.

### Expected

* The final body keeps `<media:document>`.
* The final body includes an explicit unavailable reason.
* Successful document downloads remain unchanged.

### Actual

* Before this fix, failed inbound Telegram document downloads could degrade into a placeholder-only body such as `<media:document>` with no explicit reason.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

* [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
* [ ] Trace/log snippets
* [ ] Screenshot/recording
* [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

* Verified scenarios:

  * Reviewed the final reduced patch and confirmed it stays scoped to Telegram inbound document fallback messaging.
  * Ran focused local tests for resolver behavior and final body rendering.
* Edge cases checked:

  * successful document download path remains unchanged
  * file-too-large fallback reason
  * generic download-failed fallback reason
  * text + failed document preserves user text, placeholder, and reason
* What you did **not** verify:

  * live end-to-end Telegram bot reproduction

## Review Conversations

* [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
* [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

* Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
* Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
* Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
* If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

* Risk: The unavailable-reason plumbing now flows through Telegram message-context handling.

  * Mitigation: The patch is scoped to failed inbound document handling and covered by focused resolver/body tests.
* Risk: Final body formatting could accidentally drop user text or the placeholder.

  * Mitigation: Focused rendered-body coverage locks in both document-only and text + failed document output.

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Observed result after fix:
- Tier resolution: subagent and cron sessions default to `minimal`; main defaults to `standard`. Any session honors an explicit `tierOverride`.
- Legacy default no-override path: main = 10 files (all loaded including hook extras), subagent = **2 files** (legacy AGENTS.md + TOOLS.md root only — hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` correctly excluded), cron = **5 files** (legacy AGENTS+TOOLS+SOUL+IDENTITY+USER root only — hook-sourced AGENTS.md correctly excluded). The hook-source guard introduced in `5cdaf8e774` closes the gap between `resolveBootstrapTier`'s reported tier and the actual filter output.
- Explicit `tier="minimal"`: 5 files (MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="standard"`: 8 files (STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST root only). Hook-sourced files always excluded.
- Explicit `tier="full"`: 10 files (all loaded including hook extras).
- Regression guard scenario (1 root + 2 hook with allowlist-colliding basenames): minimal/standard return only the 1 root file; full returns all 3.
- Inclusion order across paths: legacy default ⊂ minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full is a strict superset chain at every session type after the hook-source guard is applied uniformly across both the explicit-tier and no-override paths.

What was not tested: glob expansion of `loadExtraBootstrapFilesWithDiagnostics` against a live filesystem (the harness supplies the `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` directly to exercise the filter under study). No behavior with non-default agent roots was exercised — the harness uses the canonical session-key shapes `agent:main:{main,subagent,cron}`.


## Testing (supplemental)

`src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` (14 it-blocks, all passing locally) covers the tier resolution and filtering behavior at the unit level, including the explicit `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion guard added with the `2a3faf3151` fix. `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` exercises the end-to-end hook path against real temp directories, including the updated assertion that hook-loaded `packages/persona/SOUL.md` does not slip into a subagent session.

## Changes

- `src/agents/workspace.ts` — `BootstrapTier` type, `MINIMAL_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `STANDARD_BOOTSTRAP_ALLOWLIST`, `resolveBootstrapTier`, and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession`. Both `minimal` and `standard` filters apply the `file.source !== "hook"` guard.
- `src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts` — `source: "root"` provenance field on the root-load path.
- `src/agents/workspace.tiered-bootstrap.test.ts` — 14 it-blocks; new minimal-tier regression added alongside the existing standard-tier one.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.ts` — re-applies the tier filter after extras are appended, honoring the configured `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.
- `src/hooks/bundled/bootstrap-extra-files/handler.test.ts` — refreshed subagent assertion to expect the corrected hook-exclusion behavior.
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` / `schema.labels.ts` / `types.agent-defaults.ts` / `zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts` — config plumbing for `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier`.

target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them - Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally - Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) - no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` - inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream - `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) - back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` - added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` - added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` - extended the existing `vi.mock("./draft-stream.js", …)` shim with a no-op `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` stub so the suite keeps wiring through the mocked draft module.

## Compatibility and exported surface (maintainer sign-off requested)

Two intentional changes need explicit maintainer acceptance before merge:

- **Visible delivery-shape change.** For `streaming.mode: "block"` and for preview-off `blockStreaming: true`, a tool-heavy turn now produces multiple visible Mattermost posts (one per text/tool boundary) instead of one repeatedly-edited post. This is the fix for #87322 (earlier blocks were being overwritten), but it is a user-visible change for operators already running those configurations. Default `streaming.mode: "partial"` and `"progress"` are unchanged, and `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.
- **Exported draft-loop surface.** This adds an optional `takePending?(): string` member to the exported `DraftStreamLoop` type (`src/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound`). It is additive and optional, so existing plugin-SDK implementors are not broken, but it is a plugin-SDK-adjacent surface change that maintainers should accept.

I cannot accept these on the maintainers' behalf; flagging them here so the delivery-shape and exported-type changes are an explicit pre-merge decision.

## Diff size

```
 docs/channels/mattermost.md                        |   6 +-
 docs/concepts/progress-drafts.md                   |  16 +-
 docs/concepts/streaming.md                         |   5 +-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts | 271 ++++++++++++++++++++-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts      |  76 ++++++-
 .../monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts           | 111 ++++++++++-
 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts    |  34 ++-
 src/channels/draft-stream-loop.ts                  |  10 +
 8 files changed, 502 insertions(+), 27 deletions(-)
```

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Pending-partial boundary seal (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:167-168`)

ClawSweeper's latest re-review flagged a deeper boundary race. When a block's first `POST /posts` create is still in flight and a later cumulative `onPartialReply` leaves text pending for the same block, `forceNewMessage` bumped `postGeneration` and cleared `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. When the create then resolved, its `gen === postGeneration` guard failed because the generation had already advanced, so the returned post id was discarded. `sealedPostId` had already been captured as `undefined`, so the pending text went out as a second `POST /posts` instead of editing the just-created post. A slow server left the user with both a stale partial post and the sealed block post.

Fix (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts`): `forceNewMessage` takes the pending seal text first (cancelling the throttle timer), then synchronously bumps `postGeneration` and clears the live `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. Each create now records its post id keyed by the generation it was issued under (`createdPostIdsByGeneration`), so after the await the boundary recovers the finished block's real post id from that record and edits it with the pending cumulative text instead of creating a duplicate. Clearing the cursor before the await (rather than after) is what also makes the detach synchronous; see the next section for why that ordering is load-bearing under fire-and-forget callbacks.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it`): queues a second cumulative partial while the first create is held in flight, then fires the boundary and asserts the request sequence is `POST /posts` then `PUT /posts/post-1` (one create, one in-place edit). The previous ordering produced `['/posts', '/posts']` (two creates, the duplicate ClawSweeper flagged); the fix produces `['/posts', '/posts/post-1']`. The generation-keyed record is what lets the seal still find the just-created post id even though the generation is bumped before the await.

Block-mode tool-progress test correction (`monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`): the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives three `bash` progress events with distinct commands and `detailMode: "raw"`. Bare same-tool progress with hidden detail renders to an identical preview line, and the progress compositor correctly dedups identical text to a single update, so the prior version asserted three preview updates against one rendered line. With distinct rendered lines the case now exercises its real intent: three in-place updates on one post, no boundary for same-tool progress, and exactly one boundary when a new tool starts.

## Synchronous boundary detach (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:165-168`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review made the load-bearing point that the embedded-agent subscriber does not await the boundary callback. `onPartialReply`/`onReasoningStream` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:281,1193`) and `onAssistantMessageStart`/`onReasoningEnd` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:144,573,687`) are all dispatched with `void`. So the boundary callback's async tail is not sequenced before the next partial. With the previous ordering, `forceNewMessage` awaited the in-flight op *before* clearing `streamPostId`, which meant the live post id stayed attached to the finished block across the await. A partial for the next block that arrived during that await - flushing immediately once the throttle window was open - would read the still-attached `streamPostId` and `PUT` its text onto the previous block's post, overwriting it. Whether the overwrite happened depended on microtask ordering between the boundary's clear and the partial's flush, which is exactly the kind of "incorrect under the real caller" the finding called out.

Fix: `forceNewMessage` now bumps the generation and clears `streamPostId` synchronously, before it awaits anything. The instant the boundary fires, the live cursor is detached, so any fire-and-forget partial that arrives next deterministically opens a fresh post regardless of microtask ordering. The seal onto the previous block's in-flight create is preserved via the generation-keyed record described above. This is the "detach synchronously" option from the review.

Real behavior proof (live HTTP, fire-and-forget interleave): a loopback Mattermost v4 server (`POST /api/v4/posts` artificially held 120ms) driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` over real `fetch` round-trips. Block #1 creates `post-1`; the throttle window is allowed to elapse; the boundary is then fired fire-and-forget (`void forceNewMessage()`, not awaited, as the embedded subscriber does), and block #2's partial arrives next. The same script was run against the committed pre-fix source (`56186cfe25`) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `56186cfe25`):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = "post-1"
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-1  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 1  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
=> block #1 post was OVERWRITTEN by block #2 (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = undefined
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Looking into the logs now..."
=> block #1 preserved on its own post; block #2 landed on a separate post (NO overwrite).
```

The one observable that flips the outcome is `stream.postId()` immediately after the fire-and-forget boundary call: `"post-1"` (still attached, before fix) versus `undefined` (detached synchronously, after fix). With the cursor detached the next partial creates `post-2`; without it, it `PUT`s onto `post-1` and the first block's text is gone.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles`): fires `forceNewMessage()` without awaiting it, asserts `postId()` is already `undefined`, then updates with the next block's text and asserts two `POST /posts` creates (`post-1`, `post-2`) rather than an edit of `post-1`. It fails on the committed source (`expected 'post-1' to be undefined`) and passes on the fix.

## Boundary gate clobber (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:78`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review found the matching race one level up, in the gate. `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` left `hasStreamedContent` true while it awaited `forceNewMessage()`, then reset it to false after the await. Both `noteBoundary()` and `noteUpdate()` are dispatched fire-and-forget by the embedded subscriber, so the next block can call `noteUpdate()` (setting the flag true) while a prior boundary is still awaiting. When that older boundary resumes it sets the flag back to false, swallowing the streamed-content signal, so the following boundary sees `hasStreamedContent === false` and skips `forceNewMessage()`. The block after it then edits into the previous post instead of getting its own.

Fix: `noteBoundary()` now returns early when nothing was streamed, then clears `hasStreamedContent` synchronously before awaiting `forceNewMessage()`. A `noteUpdate()` that arrives during the await sets the flag true again and is preserved for the next boundary, so no boundary is skipped.

Real behavior proof through the actual embedded callback path (current head): rather than calling the controller directly, this proof drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) and lets it dispatch `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onPartialReply` with its real `void` fire-and-forget semantics. Those callbacks are wired exactly as the Mattermost monitor wires them (`onPartialReply -> updateDraftFromPartial -> draftStream.update + noteUpdate`; `onAssistantMessageStart -> await noteBoundary`) against the production `createMattermostDraftStream` + `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` over the loopback Mattermost v4 server. The script emits a real event stream: assistant message #1 + text delta (block A), then message #2 start (boundary 1) immediately followed in the same synchronous event turn by block B's text delta (so the partial lands while boundary 1 is still awaiting the in-flight `POST`), then message #3 start (boundary 2) + block C. Run against the committed head (`c8c90e0df8`, gate unfixed) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `c8c90e0df8`, buggy gate):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-2  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 2
=> only 2 posts: a boundary was skipped and a block was overwritten (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-3  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 3  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block B: found the bad commit"
   post-3 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 3
=> all three blocks preserved on separate posts (real embedded callback ordering).
```

Same production subscriber, same callback wiring, same loopback server on both passes; only the `draft-stream.ts` source differs. Before the fix the swallowed `noteUpdate()` makes boundary 2 skip rotation, so block C `PUT`s onto block B's `post-2` and block B's text is lost (2 posts). After the fix boundary 2 rotates and block C lands on `post-3` (3 posts). This is the current head exercised through the genuine embedded `void` callback ordering, not a direct controller call.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending`): holds `forceNewMessage()` in flight for the first boundary, calls `noteUpdate()` during that await, releases, then fires a second boundary and asserts `forceNewMessage` is called twice. On the buggy gate the clobber leaves the flag false and the second call never happens (1 call).

Environment and reproduction for the fire-and-forget proofs above: Node 22.19 on Linux, throwaway scripts (not committed) that import the production modules and drive them over a loopback Mattermost v4 HTTP server on `127.0.0.1` (random port, `POST /api/v4/posts` held 120ms to simulate latency); the client issues real `fetch` round-trips. The gate-clobber proof additionally drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` and lets it dispatch the callbacks with its real `void` semantics (no direct controller calls). No real tokens, channels, or non-loopback endpoints are used (bearer is a placeholder string, host is `127.0.0.1`). Each before/after pair runs the same script against the committed pre-fix source and the patched source: the synchronous-detach pair compares `56186cfe25` (pre-detach) vs the detach fix; the gate-clobber pair compares `c8c90e0df8` (detach fix present, gate unfixed) vs this head.

## Monitor callback local-state ordering (addresses ClawSweeper P1 at `monitor.ts:1950`)

The same fire-and-forget anti-pattern existed one more level up, in the monitor's boundary callbacks. `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onReasoningEnd` awaited `previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` and only afterward reset the per-turn local state (`lastPartialText = ""`, `progressDraft.reset()`). Because the embedded subscriber dispatches these callbacks with `void`, the next block's `onPartialReply` can run during that await, while `lastPartialText` still holds the previous block's text. `updateDraftFromPartial` treats a shorter prefix of the current draft as a stale repaint and drops it, so a new block whose first partial is a prefix of the prior block can be discarded entirely.

Fix (`monitor.ts`): both callbacks now start the split, reset the local state synchronously, then await: `const boundarySettled = previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary(); lastPartialText = ""; progressDraft.reset(); ... await boundarySettled;`. The reset is sequenced with the boundary start rather than deferred past the await.

Real behavior proof through the embedded path (same `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` harness, current head, only the `onAssistantMessageStart` ordering toggled to mirror the monitor before/after): block A streams a long line whose `POST` is still in flight; the boundary fires; block B's only partial is `"Reverting"`, a prefix of block A's `"Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"`, and arrives during the boundary await.

```
===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = OLD (await, then reset) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   distinct posts: 1
   => block B was DROPPED: its prefix partial was deduped against stale block-A text during the boundary await.

===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = NEW (start split, reset synchronously, then await) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   post-2 message="Reverting"
   distinct posts: 2
   => block B preserved on its own post.
```

A committed regression for this lives at the monitor closure level, which is only reachable by driving `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (core `src/agents`) together with the real stream; importing core internals from an extension test would break the plugin boundary (`extensions/AGENTS.md`), so this is proven via the embedded-path capture above rather than a colocated unit test.

## Real behavior proof (feature-level: block rotation)

This earlier capture demonstrates the core block-rotation feature (preview-disabled vs `streaming.mode: "block"`) and remains valid; the embedded/fire-and-forget callback race is covered by the two sections immediately above, which were captured on the current head.

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) - including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` - driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn - `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated - first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message - 
```
[23:57:48.660] Fake Mattermost server listening on http://127.0.0.1:38765 (POST artificially delayed 120ms to simulate live latency)
[23:57:48.661]
===== BEFORE FIX (streaming.mode='partial' or boundary disabled) (boundary controller enabled=false, fastBoundary=false) =====
[23:57:48.661]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.661] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.731] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-1 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:48.861] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.862] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:48.863] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.864] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:48.865] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.865] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:48.866] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.867] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."
[23:57:48.918]
===== AFTER FIX (streaming.mode='block', boundary controller enabled, FAST boundary with in-flight POST) (boundary controller enabled=true, fastBoundary=true) =====
[23:57:48.918]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY - fast, POST still in flight]
[23:57:48.918]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:48.919] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-2 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.041] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.042] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-3 message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.164] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.166] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-4 message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.290] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.292] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-5 message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:49.415]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.417] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-6 message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."

===== SUMMARY =====
[23:57:49.540] BEFORE: 1 POST /posts (creates), 4 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] AFTER : 5 POST /posts (creates), 0 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] => AFTER produces 5 distinct Mattermost posts; BEFORE collapses to a single post with 4 in-place edits, which is the issue #87322 user-visible symptom.
```

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create - `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary - five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done - revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees - earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence - only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

Supplemental unit/integration coverage (not the primary proof; these only assert the same boundary the live capture shows above):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` → 20/20 passed (includes the `forceNewMessage` regression, the `flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post` delayed-transport regression, the `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it` regression, the `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles` synchronous-detach regression, the new `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending` gate-clobber regression, and 5 controller cases).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` → 10/10 passed (after extending the existing draft-stream module mock with a no-op controller stub; the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives distinct rendered progress lines).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` → 29 files / 377/377 passed (full mattermost dir suite, includes `monitor.test.ts` and `accounts.test.ts`).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` / `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm check:test-types` → all exit 0.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files → exit 0.
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here - that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 - overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

PR surface: Source +135, Tests +128, Docs +25, Generated 0. Total +288 across 8 files.

target.title: feat(agents): expose tools and their schemas via new after_tools_resolved hook [AI-assisted]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** Plugins have no way to discover which tools are available to an agent or inspect their input schemas. The plugin API exposes `registerTool()` but no `listTools()` or `getTools()`. Built-in tools (exec, read, write, web_fetch, etc.) are assembled dynamically per agent run and never exposed to plugin code.
- **Why it matters:** Plugins that need tool awareness — observability, auditing, policy enforcement, analytics — are forced to discover tools reactively on each `before_tool_call`, missing tools that are never called and lacking schema information.
- **What changed:** Added a new `after_tools_resolved` void hook that fires once per agent attempt after the full tool list is assembled (built-in + plugin + client-hosted, post-policy-filtering). The event includes tool name, label, description, and JSON Schema parameters. Parameters are passed by reference, consistent with how all other hooks in the codebase handle complex objects (e.g., `messages` in `llm_input`, `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No existing hooks or tool assembly logic modified. Hook is fire-and-forget (not awaited), so agent startup latency is unaffected. Not fired during compaction runs.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR
* https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/21330
* https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/discussions/26940

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New plugin hook `after_tools_resolved` available via `api.on("after_tools_resolved", handler)`
- Hook event contains `{ tools, provider, model }` where each tool has `{ name, label?, description?, parameters? }`
- Parameters are passed by reference (consistent with all other hooks — no defensive cloning)
- Hook context includes `agentId`, `sessionKey`, `sessionId`, `workspaceDir`
- Fire-and-forget: plugin handler errors are caught and logged, never block agent startup

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — read-only observability hook, no new capabilities granted
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` — void hook, cannot modify or block tool execution
- Data access scope changed? `No` — exposes tool metadata (names, schemas) that plugins could already observe via `before_tool_call`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu (remote VM)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 22
- Model/provider: Anthropic / claude-opus-4-6
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp
- Relevant config: Default agent with full tool profile

### Steps

1. Create a workspace plugin at `{workspaceDir}/.openclaw/extensions/test-tools-hook/`:

   `openclaw.plugin.json`:
   ```json
   {
     "id": "test-tools-hook",
     "configSchema": {
       "type": "object",
       "additionalProperties": false,
       "properties": {}
     }
   }
   ```

   `index.ts`:
   ```typescript
   export default function testToolsHook(api) {
     api.on("after_tools_resolved", async (event, ctx) => {
       const toolNames = event.tools.map((t) => t.name).join(", ");
       console.log(`[test-tools-hook] agent=${ctx.agentId} provider=${event.provider} model=${event.model}`);
       console.log(`[test-tools-hook] ${event.tools.length} tools resolved: ${toolNames}`);
       for (const tool of event.tools) {
         const params = tool.parameters?.properties
           ? Object.keys(tool.parameters.properties).join(", ")
           : "(no schema)";
         console.log(`  - ${tool.name}: ${params}`);
       }
     });
   }
   ```

2. Restart the gateway: `openclaw gateway --force --verbose`
3. Send a WhatsApp message to trigger an agent run

### Expected

- Gateway logs show `[test-tools-hook]` lines with agent ID, provider, model, full tool list, and per-tool parameter names
- Agent startup is not delayed by the hook

### Actual

- Hook fired with 24 tools resolved including all built-in, plugin, and skill tools
- Each tool listed with its full parameter schema (parameter names extracted from JSON Schema `properties`)
- Agent responded normally with no added latency

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

**Live gateway log output (WhatsApp inbound → hook fires → agent responds):**

```
04:55:20 [test-tools-hook] agent=main provider=anthropic model=claude-opus-4-6
04:55:20 [test-tools-hook] 24 tools resolved: read, edit, write, exec, process, browser, canvas, nodes, cron, message, tts, gateway, agents_list, sessions_list, sessions_history, sessions_send, sessions_spawn, subagents, session_status, web_search, web_fetch, image, memory_search, memory_get
04:55:20   - read: path, offset, limit, file_path
04:55:20   - edit: path, oldText, newText, file_path, old_string, new_string
04:55:20   - write: path, content, file_path
04:55:20   - exec: command, workdir, env, yieldMs, background, timeout, pty, elevated, host, security, ask, node
04:55:20   - process: action, sessionId, data, keys, hex, literal, text, bracketed, eof, offset, limit, timeout
04:55:20   - browser: action, target, node, profile, targetUrl, targetId, limit, maxChars, mode, snapshotFormat, refs, interactive, compact, depth, selector, frame, labels, fullPage, ref, element, type, level, paths, inputRef, timeoutMs, accept, promptText, request
04:55:20   - canvas: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, node, target, x, y, width, height, url, javaScript, outputFormat, maxWidth, quality, delayMs, jsonl, jsonlPath
04:55:20   - nodes: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, node, requestId, title, body, sound, priority, delivery, facing, maxWidth, quality, delayMs, deviceId, duration, durationMs, includeAudio, fps, screenIndex, outPath, maxAgeMs, locationTimeoutMs, desiredAccuracy, command, cwd, env, commandTimeoutMs, invokeTimeoutMs, needsScreenRecording, invokeCommand, invokeParamsJson
04:55:20   - cron: action, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, includeDisabled, job, jobId, id, patch, text, mode, runMode, contextMessages
04:55:20   - message: action, channel, target, targets, accountId, dryRun, message, effectId, effect, media, filename, buffer, contentType, mimeType, caption, path, filePath, replyTo, threadId, asVoice, silent, quoteText, bestEffort, gifPlayback, messageId, emoji, remove, targetAuthor, targetAuthorUuid, groupId, limit, before, after, around, fromMe, includeArchived, ...
04:55:20   - tts: text, channel
04:55:20   - gateway: action, delayMs, reason, gatewayUrl, gatewayToken, timeoutMs, raw, baseHash, sessionKey, note, restartDelayMs
04:55:20   - agents_list:
04:55:20   - sessions_list: kinds, limit, activeMinutes, messageLimit
04:55:20   - sessions_history: sessionKey, limit, includeTools
04:55:20   - sessions_send: sessionKey, label, agentId, message, timeoutSeconds
04:55:20   - sessions_spawn: task, label, agentId, model, thinking, runTimeoutSeconds, timeoutSeconds, cleanup

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: fix(msteams): forward messageBack card actions (Action.Submit) to agent (#60952)
target.body:
## Summary

Forward `Action.Submit` messageBack activities to the agent instead of dropping them as empty messages. Closes #60952.

When a user clicks an `Action.Submit` button on an Adaptive Card in Teams, the click is sent as a messageBack activity (`type="message"` with empty text and a `value` object). The message handler strips mentions, finds empty text, and drops the message with "skipping empty message after stripping mentions." The value payload is never forwarded to the agent.

This change adds a check for `activity.value` before the empty-text drop at line 452 of `message-handler.ts`. If the value payload is a non-empty object, it serializes it as `[CARD_ACTION] {json}` so the agent receives the button payload. This is consistent with:

- The `adaptiveCard/action` invoke handling added in #60431 (which handles `Action.Execute`)
- The `fileConsent/invoke` handling added in #64087

The detection pattern: `activity.type === "message" && !text && activity.value && typeof activity.value === "object"`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send an Adaptive Card with `Action.Submit` buttons to a Teams DM
- [ ] Click a button — verify the agent receives `[CARD_ACTION] {"key":"value"}` as the message body
- [ ] Verify normal text messages still work (no regression)
- [ ] Verify empty messages are still dropped (no spurious forwarding)
- [ ] Verify `Action.Execute` invoke path (#60431) is unaffected

## Context

This is the companion fix to #55384/#60431 (Action.Execute). Together they cover both invoke formats Teams uses for Adaptive Card interactions. We've been running a sidecar HTTP proxy workaround for this since March 2026 — this fix eliminates the need for that sidecar for card actions.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(agents): release bundle MCP runtime on mid-run session reset
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `resetReplyRunSession` rotated the active `sessionId` after auto-compaction failure, context overflow, or role-ordering conflicts, but it never released the previous session id's entry from the bundle MCP runtime cache.
- Why it matters: `runtimesBySessionId` in `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts` holds `Client` -> `Transport` -> stdio `ChildProcess` references with no TTL/LRU, so old MCP workers like `gate-mcp`, `bnbchain-mcp`, and `chrome-devtools-mcp` stayed alive and accumulated until memory-constrained deployments OOM'd.
- What changed: `resetReplyRunSession` now disposes the previous session id's bundle MCP runtime in the background with `void ... .catch(...)` after the replacement session is fully established, and logs disposal failures through the existing `deps.error(...)` seam without blocking the retry path.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not alter how the new session is established, and it does not add broader cache eviction or runtime lifecycle changes outside the session-reset path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #64169
- Related #60656
- Related #62026
- Related #62731
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: mid-run session rotation persisted the new session metadata and optionally deleted the old transcript, but it never released the previous session id from the bundle MCP runtime cache.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the reset-path tests did not assert MCP runtime disposal or cover disposal failures.
- Contributing context (if known): the cache has no TTL/LRU and holds stdio-backed MCP client/process references, so each reset could strand another worker pool.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-session-reset.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: resetting a reply run session disposes the previous session runtime, still succeeds when disposal throws, and still deletes the old transcript when `cleanupTranscripts: true` even if disposal fails.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is owned by `resetReplyRunSession`, and the relevant failure handling is exposed through that helper's dependency seam.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none before this change.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[reset trigger] -> [new session metadata persisted] -> [old MCP runtime remains cached/alive]

After:
[reset trigger] -> [new session metadata persisted] -> [background dispose of old MCP runtime] -> [retry continues]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): MCP stdio runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Trigger `resetReplyRunSession` from an auto-compaction failure, context overflow, or role-ordering conflict while a bundle MCP runtime is cached for the current session id.
2. Observe that the helper rotates `sessionId`, persists the replacement session metadata, and optionally deletes old transcript files.
3. Verify that the previous session id's MCP runtime is disposed in the background and that disposal failures are logged without preventing the reset from succeeding.

### Expected

- Old session MCP workers are released when the session resets.
- Reset still returns success even if runtime disposal throws.
- Transcript cleanup still occurs when requested.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this change.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Mid-run reply-session resets should retire the old session id's bundle MCP runtime so the old session runtime cache entry cannot keep stdio MCP workers alive after the reset.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux local OpenClaw source checkout, Node with tsx, using the real bundle MCP runtime manager plus the real `resetReplyRunSession` helper. The probe used a temp session store/transcript under `/tmp` and `mcp.servers: {}` so it could prove the runtime-cache retirement path without exposing private MCP server config.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime`, `getSessionMcpRuntimeManager`, and `resetReplyRunSession`. The command cached `proof-old-session`, invoked a compaction-failure reset with an active session store and follow-up run, then polled `manager.listSessionIds()` for the old session id.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
before reset cached sessions: proof-old-session
Config was last written by a newer OpenClaw (2026.5.5); current version is 2026.5.3.
proof reset proof-old-session -> 46b164d1-4cc8-4f2f-9f79-abc9424aac7b
reset returned: true
new active session id differs: true
followup session id updated: true
old session cached after reset: false
cached sessions after reset: (none)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The old session id was present in the real bundle MCP runtime cache before reset and absent after reset; the reset still returned `true`, the active session id rotated, and the follow-up run was rebound to the new session id.
- **What was not tested**: Long-running production RSS over multiple days and teardown of a private third-party stdio MCP server process tree were not exercised in this local probe.

## Evidence

- Edge cases checked: disposal success, disposal failure logging, and transcript cleanup when disposal fails.
- What you did **not** verify: long-running production memory behavior outside the targeted reset/test surface.

- Backward compatible? (Yes)

- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

- Risk: background disposal could fail or hang independently of the retry path.
  - Mitigation: disposal is intentionally non-blocking, failures are logged through `deps.error(...)`, and tests cover the failure path plus transcript cleanup continuity.

## Tests

target.title: feat: Provider circuit breaker for quota exhaustion
target.body:
Resolves #64085

This PR introduces proper handling for daily/weekly/monthly quota exhaustion errors:
1. Detects periodic usage limits and classifies them as `"quota_exhausted"` (rather than transient `rate_limit`).
2. Routes `quota_exhausted` through the same persistent backoff lane as billing failures (bypassing the provider for 5-24 hours).
3. Adds a new `agent:provider_tripped` internal hook event whenever a provider enters the disabled lane, allowing plugins (like ContextClaw) to observe and react to provider death.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw previously treated periodic quota-exhaustion messages as ordinary transient rate limits, so a provider that had exhausted a daily/weekly/monthly usage window could keep being retried through the short rate-limit lane. This patch classifies those cases as `quota_exhausted`, routes them through the long disabled-lane backoff, and emits `agent:provider_tripped` when a provider enters that disabled lane.

Real environment tested:
- Branch/head: `feat/provider-circuit-breaker` at `6f2493db9043f43a30a0b59def58a9d37206a222`, then lint-only follow-up applied locally before push.
- Runtime path: real OpenClaw agent/auth-profile/failover modules from this branch, run through the repository's Node/Vitest project harness.
- Before-patch incident context: ScientificProgrammer reported a real OpenClaw deployment where a Gemini monthly spending-cap condition produced repeated `RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED` cascades across 26 runIds and about 390 exhausted-provider events.

Exact steps or command run after the patch:
- Ran `npm run format:check -- src/agents/auth-profiles/state-observation.ts`.
- Ran `pnpm lint --threads=8`.

```text

pnpm lint --threads=8: passed
focused runtime-path suite:

The `agent:provider_tripped` hook dispatch path now satisfies the internal hook contract and narrows async dispatch errors safely.

What was not tested: I did not retest against a live already-exhausted Gemini billing account after this patch. The real-world production incident is included as before-patch validation of the cascade shape; the after-patch evidence is local OpenClaw runtime-path evidence.
